text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY TRANSPLANT CENTER
ALLOGENEIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT CONSULTATION AND PRE-TRANSPLANT EVALUATION

ATTENDING: Bone Marrow Transplant Program
VISIT TYPE: New Patient Consultation / Pre-Transplant Evaluation

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Chen is a 48-year-old woman with newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia (AML), FLT3-ITD positive, who presents to the transplant center for pre-transplant evaluation and consent for matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT). She was referred by her treating hematologist/oncologist following achievement of first complete remission after one cycle of 7+3 induction chemotherapy with midostaurin.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a non-smoking high school biology teacher who approximately 7 weeks ago presented to an outside institution with a 3-week history of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, spontaneous gingival bleeding, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss. Initial CBC was striking for a WBC of 87,400/uL with circulating blasts comprising approximately 40% of the differential, hemoglobin of 6.8 g/dL, and a platelet count of 32,000/uL. Physical examination at presentation was notable for pallor, petechiae on bilateral lower extremities, prominent gingival hyperplasia with active oozing, and a spleen tip palpable approximately 4 cm below the left costal margin. ECOG performance status was 1 at diagnosis.

Bone marrow biopsy and aspirate at diagnosis showed markedly hypercellular marrow (estimated cellularity 95%) with 72% myeloblasts, with Auer rods identified on peripheral smear. Immunophenotyping by flow cytometry was consistent with AML, NOS: blasts were CD34+, CD117+, CD33+, CD13+, MPO+, HLA-DR+ with dim CD7; negative for CD19, CD3, CD56, CD14, CD64. Conventional karyotype showed a normal female karyotype (46,XX) in 20/20 metaphases, and FISH panel was negative for t(8;21), inv(16), and t(15;17). Comprehensive myeloid NGS (523-gene panel) was subsequently resulted and demonstrated the following clinically relevant mutations: FLT3-ITD (exon 14, 33 bp insertion, allelic ratio 0.68, VAF 40.2%), DNMT3A p.R882H (VAF 48.1%), RUNX1 p.S291fs*16 (VAF 43.7%), TET2 p.Q916* (VAF 22.4%), and WT1 p.R462Q (VAF 18.9%); no NPM1, IDH1/IDH2, CEBPA, TP53, ASXL1, or NRAS/KRAS mutations were identified. ELN 2022 risk classification is Adverse, driven by the FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio in the NPM1-unmutated setting compounded by the RUNX1 frameshift mutation.

Pre-treatment imaging included a CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis which showed mild splenomegaly (14.2 cm), mild hepatomegaly (17.1 cm), and small bilateral pleural effusions, interpreted as leukemic infiltration; no discrete extramedullary masses were identified. Brain MRI showed no CNS involvement.

She was admitted to the inpatient hematology unit and received a single cycle of standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy: cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day by continuous IV infusion on days 1 through 7, daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV on days 1 through 3, and midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily on days 8 through 21 per the RATIFY protocol. Pre-treatment echocardiogram demonstrated LVEF of 62% with no significant valvular disease. Her hospitalization was complicated by febrile neutropenia on approximately day 10 (ANC 0/uL), with CT chest showing bilateral lower lobe consolidations concerning for pneumonia; she was empirically treated with cefepime and micafungin for 14 days, defervescing after 5 days without bacteremia identified. She also developed grade 2 oral mucositis requiring IV analgesics, and grade 2 nausea/vomiting attributed to midostaurin (managed with addition of prochlorperazine) with transient grade 1 transaminase elevation that resolved spontaneously.

Day 28 bone marrow biopsy showed a hypocellular marrow (cellularity 10-15%) with trilineage hematopoiesis, myeloblasts at 3%, and MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry (below 0.01% threshold). FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A R882H were not detected on post-induction molecular testing. This constitutes a morphologic complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity after a single induction cycle — an excellent early response.

Given the adverse ELN risk stratification (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, RUNX1 mutation, DNMT3A co-mutation in an NPM1-unmutated background), the decision was made in accordance with current guidelines and multidisciplinary review to proceed directly to allogeneic stem cell transplant in first CR without intervening consolidation chemotherapy, with the goal of expediting transplant. HLA typing was performed on the patient and a single full sibling, who was found to be a 7/10 HLA match and is therefore not a suitable related donor. Concurrent unrelated donor search through the National Marrow Donor Program identified a 10/10 HLA-matched (A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1 allele-level matching) unrelated male donor, age 32, CMV seropositive. The patient is herself CMV seropositive.

A pre-transplant restaging PET-CT of the whole body performed approximately 6 weeks post-diagnosis showed no FDG-avid lesions anywhere, with improvement in spleen size to 11.8 cm and normalization of liver size to 15.3 cm, consistent with complete metabolic response and ongoing remission. No evidence of extramedullary disease.

Ms. Chen presents today for formal pre-transplant evaluation, organ function assessment, and informed consent for myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) followed by infusion of peripheral blood stem cells from the 10/10 MUD.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Hypothyroidism — managed with levothyroxine, well-controlled on stable dosing
2. AML, NOS, FLT3-ITD positive (high allelic ratio), adverse ELN 2022 risk — as detailed above; diagnosed approximately 7 weeks ago
3. No prior hematologic conditions
4. No prior malignancy
5. No prior chemotherapy or radiation prior to current diagnosis
6. No prior cardiac disease; LVEF 62% on pre-induction echocardiogram
7. Bilateral lower lobe pneumonia (during induction, likely fungal or bacterial) — treated and resolved

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- No prior surgeries reported

---

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Chen is a married woman who lives at home with her husband, who is her primary caregiver and has been present throughout her treatment course. She is a high school biology teacher and was working full-time prior to her diagnosis. She is a lifelong non-smoker. She denies alcohol use. She denies illicit drug use. No significant environmental or occupational toxic exposures identified. She has no history of prior radiation exposure. Her husband is actively engaged and supportive; a formal psychosocial evaluation has been completed and confirmed adequate caregiver and social support systems. She lives within a reasonable distance of the transplant center to fulfill post-transplant outpatient monitoring requirements.

---

FAMILY HISTORY:
- No known family history of hematologic malignancies
- No known family history of AML, MDS, or related myeloid disorders
- Father: deceased, cause not specified
- Mother: alive, no known malignancy
- One full sibling (HLA-typed, 7/10 match, not suitable as donor)
- No known hereditary cancer syndromes
- No known familial bone marrow failure syndromes (Fanconi anemia, dyskeratosis congenita, etc.)

---

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies
- No known food allergies
- No known environmental allergies

---

MEDICATIONS (at time of this visit):
1. Levothyroxine — dose per endocrinology (chronic, pre-existing)
2. Acyclovir — HSV/VZV prophylaxis (post-induction)
3. Fluconazole — antifungal prophylaxis
4. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole — PJP prophylaxis
5. No tacrolimus at this time (not yet started)
6. Letermovir — not yet started (planned post-transplant)
7. Midostaurin — completed per protocol (days 8-21 of induction)

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: No fevers, chills, or night sweats. Appetite returning to near baseline. Reports mild ongoing fatigue, significantly improved compared to presentation. Has regained approximately 6 of the 12 pounds lost prior to diagnosis. No further weight loss.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Gingival hyperplasia and bleeding have completely resolved. No oral ulcers at this visit. Mild residual xerostomia, thought to be related to prior chemotherapy. No visual changes.
Respiratory: No cough. No dyspnea at rest or with exertion. No pleuritic chest pain. Bilateral lower lobe infiltrates from induction hospitalization clinically resolved.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain. No palpitations. No lower extremity edema. No orthopnea or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite good. No nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain. Normal bowel habits, no diarrhea, no blood in stool.
Genitourinary: Normal urinary function. No hematuria.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain. No arthralgias or myalgias.
Neurologic: No headaches, vision changes, focal weakness, numbness, or tingling. No confusion.
Dermatologic: No rashes. No petechiae or ecchymoses at this time (counts recovered).
Endocrine: Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine.
Psychiatric: Anxious about the upcoming transplant but reports coping adequately with support from husband and social work team. No active depression. Sleeping adequately.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Afebrile. Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg. Heart rate 78 bpm and regular. Respiratory rate 16 breaths/min. SpO2 98% on room air. Height 163 cm. Weight 58.4 kg (BSA ~1.63 m2).

General: Alert, oriented, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Appears less pale than at presentation. Good affect. Engaged in discussion of transplant details.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae pink — no pallor. Oropharynx clear. Gingival tissue normal without hyperplasia or active bleeding. Mucous membranes moist. No oral ulcers or thrush.

Lymphatics: No palpable cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 normal. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. Peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetric.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Good air entry throughout. No dullness to percussion at the bases.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly on palpation (liver edge not appreciated below costal margin). Spleen no longer palpable below the left costal margin (previously 4 cm). No ascites. Normal bowel sounds.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No petechiae or ecchymoses. No erythema or induration at prior IV sites.

Skin: No rashes, no active lesions.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. No focal deficits. Cranial nerves grossly intact.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (reviewed from recent studies):
CBC (most recent, approximately 5 weeks post-induction):
- WBC 3.1 k/uL → improving, now closer to 4-5 k/uL range on recent outpatient checks
- Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL → transfusion-independent
- Platelets 88,000/uL → recovering, transfusion-independent
- ANC: recovering

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (today):
- Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, Chloride 103 mEq/L
- BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL — eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2 (excellent renal function)
- Total Bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Direct Bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL
- ALT 28 U/L, AST 24 U/L — within normal limits (prior grade 1 transaminase elevation resolved)
- Alkaline Phosphatase 72 U/L
- Albumin 3.8 g/dL
- Total Protein 6.9 g/dL

Coagulation studies: PT 12.1 sec, INR 1.0, PTT 28 sec — normal

Thyroid function: TSH within normal limits on current levothyroxine dose

MRD by flow cytometry (day 28 post-induction): Negative (below 0.01% threshold)
FLT3-ITD by PCR (day 28 post-induction): Not detected

Infectious serologies:
- CMV IgG: positive (seropositive)
- EBV IgG: positive
- HSV IgG: positive
- Toxoplasma IgG: negative
- Hepatitis B surface antigen: negative; Hepatitis B core antibody: negative; Hepatitis B surface antibody: positive (immune)
- Hepatitis C antibody: negative
- HIV 1/2: negative
- RPR: non-reactive

HLA typing: Patient — confirmed 10/10 match with identified MUD (allele-level: HLA-A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1)

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed):
PET-CT whole body (pre-transplant restaging, approximately 6 weeks post-diagnosis): No FDG-avid lesions identified in the neck, chest, abdomen, or pelvis. No lymphadenopathy. Spleen has decreased from 14.2 cm at diagnosis to 11.8 cm — nearly back to normal size. Liver has normalized from 17.1 cm to 15.3 cm. No bone lesions. Findings are consistent with complete metabolic response and ongoing remission with no evidence of residual or extramedullary disease.

---

ORGAN FUNCTION ASSESSMENT FOR TRANSPLANT ELIGIBILITY:

Cardiac: Repeat echocardiogram performed today. LVEF 60% (stable, adequate). No significant valvular disease. No pericardial effusion. EKG: normal sinus rhythm, no significant conduction abnormalities. She completed a single cycle of anthracycline (daunorubicin, total cumulative dose ~98 mg/m2); cardiac function well-preserved.

Pulmonary: Pulmonary function tests performed today:
- FEV1: 88% of predicted
- FVC: 90% of predicted
- FEV1/FVC ratio: normal
- DLCO (corrected): 82% of predicted
Interpretation: No significant obstructive or restrictive ventilatory defect. DLCO mildly reduced but above the 70% threshold for transplant eligibility. Adequate for myeloablative conditioning.

Renal: Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2 — excellent.

Hepatic: Transaminases, bilirubin within normal limits. Albumin 3.8 g/dL. No signs of hepatic dysfunction.

Performance Status: ECOG PS 0-1 (walks independently, performs most activities of daily living, working part-time from home). KPS approximately 80-90%.

Dental Clearance: Completed. No active dental infections or sources of oral sepsis identified. Dental procedures completed prior to conditioning as appropriate.

Psychosocial Evaluation: Completed by transplant social worker. Ms. Chen has adequate caregiver support (husband), appropriate housing, transportation, financial resources, and health literacy. She demonstrates clear understanding of the transplant process and realistic expectations regarding recovery. No psychiatric contraindications identified. She expresses appropriate anxiety which is manageable.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old woman with newly diagnosed FLT3-ITD-positive AML (high allelic ratio, NPM1-unmutated, RUNX1-mutated, DNMT3A-mutated), ELN 2022 Adverse risk, who achieved a first complete remission with MRD negativity after a single cycle of 7+3 induction with midostaurin. She is now presenting in excellent condition for pre-transplant evaluation. All organ function assessments are satisfactory for myeloablative allogeneic transplant. A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor has been identified.

1. TRANSPLANT INDICATION AND STRATEGY:
Allogeneic SCT in first CR is strongly indicated and supported by current ELN 2022 guidelines and published data for patients with adverse-risk AML. The combination of FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio in an NPM1-unmutated setting, compounded by the presence of RUNX1 frameshift and DNMT3A R882H co-mutations, confers a very high risk of relapse with chemotherapy alone, estimated at >70-80% without consolidative transplant. The achievement of MRD negativity prior to transplant is an excellent prognostic feature and should be carried into the transplant. Allogeneic transplant offers both ablation of residual disease and the graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect, which is especially important in this setting.

Donor: 10/10 HLA-matched (allele-level) unrelated male donor, age 32, CMV seropositive. Donor workup is in progress. Peripheral blood stem cells will be used as the graft source (standard for MUD transplants in our program to ensure robust engraftment).

2. CONDITIONING REGIMEN:
Myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) is planned. Fludarabine 40 mg/m2 IV daily on days -6 through -3, combined with busulfan 3.2 mg/kg IV daily on days -6 through -3, pharmacokinetically dosed targeting AUC of 4000-5000 umol*min/L/day. Anti-seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam will be administered for the duration of busulfan administration.

Rationale for myeloablative (rather than reduced-intensity) conditioning: patient age (48 years), ECOG PS 0-1, preserved organ function, and adverse-risk disease all favor myeloablative conditioning to maximize disease eradication.

3. GVHD PROPHYLAXIS:
Tacrolimus (target trough 5-15 ng/mL) plus short-course MTX (15 mg/m2 on day +1; 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, +11). This is our institutional standard for MUD transplants. Post-transplant CTX was discussed but not selected given 10/10 HLA match.

4. INFECTIOUS PROPHYLAXIS:
- CMV: Letermovir 480 mg daily starting day +1 for CMV prophylaxis (both donor and recipient are CMV seropositive — high-risk pair)
- Antifungal: micafungin during neutropenic phase; transition to azole-based prophylaxis post-engraftment per protocol
- Bacterial: fluoroquinolone prophylaxis during neutropenic phase per institutional protocol
- PJP: TMP-SMX to be held during conditioning and restarted post-engraftment
- HSV/VZV: acyclovir to be continued
- PneumococcalVaccination and other post-transplant vaccinations: deferred per protocol timeline

5. FLT3 INHIBITOR MAINTENANCE POST-TRANSPLANT:
Post-transplant FLT3 inhibitor maintenance therapy will be discussed and considered after day +60 assessment pending engraftment confirmation and tolerance. Current evidence supports consideration of this approach in FLT3-ITD-positive patients post-alloHSCT, though it is not part of the current plan until engraftment and early recovery are confirmed.

6. BONE MARROW ASSESSMENT SCHEDULE:
Post-transplant bone marrow biopsy and aspirate with chimerism, MRD, and molecular studies planned at day +30 and day +100 at minimum, with additional assessments per protocol or clinical indication.

7. FERTILITY COUNSELING:
Myeloablative conditioning with busulfan is expected to cause permanent ovarian failure and infertility. Ms. Chen was counseled regarding this expected late effect. She was informed that fertility preservation options (oocyte or embryo cryopreservation) were available prior to induction but were not pursued given the urgency of treatment at diagnosis. She verbalized understanding of the expected loss of fertility. Hormone replacement therapy will be discussed post-transplant with gynecology/endocrinology as appropriate.

8. INFORMED CONSENT:
Extensive informed consent discussion conducted today in the presence of her husband. Topics covered included: goals of transplant, conditioning regimen and expected toxicities (mucositis, cytopenias, infection risk, hepatic sinusoidal obstruction syndrome/VOD — low risk with FluBu4 but discussed), acute GVHD, chronic GVHD, graft failure, secondary malignancy risk, late effects of busulfan (pulmonary, cataracts, gonadal failure), and transplant-related mortality (estimated at 10-15% for a 10/10 MUD in this age group with this conditioning). The patient and her husband asked thoughtful questions and demonstrated understanding of the risks and benefits. Written consent obtained.

9. LOGISTICS:
- Admission for conditioning anticipated to begin in approximately 1-2 weeks pending donor readiness and final donor evaluation
- Central venous catheter (tunneled/Hickman) placement will be arranged prior to admission
- Dental clearance: complete
- Psychosocial clearance: complete
- Caregiver: husband confirmed and educated
- Housing: patient lives locally; discharge planning within close proximity of transplant center confirmed

Patient is an excellent transplant candidate. She is well-prepared, in excellent functional status, in first morphologic and molecular CR, and has an optimal 10/10 MUD identified. Proceed as planned.

_________________________________
Attending, Bone Marrow Transplant Program
